metadata.title: Build a Workspace Access Audit with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. The classification of eight permissions into three always-needed, two task-specific, and three never-used made the never-used trio obvious to remove. I want to run that same audit on my own workspace and task list. Take the config and task types I'll give you and (1) list the minimum required permissions per task type, (2) classify every granted permission as always-needed, task-specific, or never-used with the task that justifies it, and (3) tell me the new blast radius once the never-used ones are removed.

props.segment: Build a Workspace Access Audit with

beats.narration_text: This is Musinique. Removing one never-used permission takes 10 seconds. Discovering you needed it takes a week.

Least-privilege is not a security policy — it's a delegation discipline. Over-permissioned workspaces make it impossible to trace which task caused a side effect, because every task had permission to cause every side effect. The audit closes the gap between what's granted and what's required.

The playlist curator who emails you 'guaranteed placement for $300' is betting you won't check. You can check in sixty seconds: paste the pitch into Claude with the right prompt, and ask it to cross-reference the playlist's follower-to-stream ratio. The scam doesn't survive the audit. You just have to run it.

artifactLines: The same tools the platforms use — pointed back at you.
Roll your own. Own the deck.

beats.narration_text: Given this workspace configuration and these task types, produce an access audit. For each task type, list the minimum required permissions. For each granted permission, classify as always-needed, task-specific, or never-used.

props.command: claude "Given this workspace configuration and these task types, produce an access audit: for each task type, list the minimum required permissions. For each granted permission, classify as: always-needed, task-specific, or never-used." < workspace_config.json

beats.narration_text: The access audit script reads the workspace config, classifies permissions per task type, and produces an audit report comparing minimum-required versus granted.

props.code:
import json
from pathlib import Path

CLASSIFICATIONS = ["always-needed", "task-specific", "never-used"]

def audit_permissions(config_path: str, task_types: list[str]) -> dict:
    config = json.loads(Path(config_path).read_text())
    granted = config.get("permissions", [])
    required_by_task = {}
    for task in task_types:
        required_by_task[task] = config.get("task_permissions", {}).get(task, [])

    all_required = set(p for perms in required_by_task.values() for p in perms)
    always_needed = [p for p in granted if all(p in required_by_task[t] for t in task_types)]
    task_specific = [p for p in granted if p in all_required and p not in always_needed]
    never_used = [p for p in granted if p not in all_required]

    return {
        "granted": len(granted),
        "always_needed": always_needed,
        "task_specific": task_specific,
        "never_used": never_used
    }
props.title: access_audit.py

beats.narration_text: 8 permissions granted. 3 always-needed: read project folder, write output folder, read CLAUDE.md. 2 task-specific: read archive folder, only needed for monthly report task. 3 never-used: write to system temp, read email folder, execute shell commands. Three permissions with no task that requires them.
Remove the 3 never-used permissions; re-run audit to confirm no task required them.

Post-removal audit: all 5 remaining permissions classified as always-needed or task-specific. No task type requires any of the three removed permissions. Blast radius of any unexpected action is now bounded by what any active task actually needs — not by everything the workspace was ever configured to allow.

The access audit maps the gap between what's permitted and what's needed. The classification — always-needed, task-specific, never-used — gives you actionable next steps. Never-used means safe to remove now. Task-specific means time-box the grant to when the task runs.
Next: capture a successful task as a reusable workflow card.
Musinique. Brutalist and Educational AI. nikbearbrown.com.
